g.data-narration: Welcome to this lecture on Assigning Object Reference Variables in Java - a fundamental concept for OOP mastery.
g.text: Assigning Object Reference Variables in Java

g.data-narration: In Java, primitive variables hold actual values while object variables store memory addresses called references.
Java memory is divided into Stack (for variables) and Heap (for objects). References in stack point to objects in heap.

svg.text: SECTION 2: Deep Dive into Reference Assignment

g.data-narration: When you assign one reference variable to another, you copy the reference, NOT the object. Both variables now point to the same object.
g.text: Box b1 = new Box(); // Creates object, b1 holds reference
Box b2 = b1; // COPIES reference - b2 now points to SAME object!

g.data-narration: This memory diagram shows aliasing - two reference variables b1 and b2 pointing to the exact same Box object in heap memory.
This code demonstrates aliasing in action. When we modify the object through b2, the change is visible through b1 too.
The == operator compares memory addresses (references), while equals() compares object contents when properly overridden.
Shallow copy creates a new object but copies references to nested objects - both original and copy share the same nested objects.
Deep copy creates completely independent objects - all nested objects are also cloned, so changes to copy don't affect original.
The this keyword is an implicit reference to the current object. It's used to distinguish instance variables from parameters and enable constructor chaining.
When passing objects to methods, Java passes a copy of the reference. The method can modify object state but cannot reassign the caller's reference.
g.text: Java is PASS-BY-VALUE (copy of reference is passed)

g.data-narration: This diagram shows pass-by-value: a copy of the reference is passed. The local parameter can modify the object but can't change what the caller points to.
When reference variables are reassigned, the original object becomes eligible for garbage collection if no references point to it.
Strings are immutable in Java. When you 'modify' a String, you actually create a new String object - a special case to remember!
g.text: String s2 = s1; // s2 also points to "Hello"

g.data-narration: Remember these essential rules: assignment copies references not objects, == compares addresses, and objects become eligible for GC when unreferenced.
For exams: Always draw memory diagrams, trace each line of code, and remember pass-by-value semantics for method calls.
g.text: EXAM TIP: Draw memory diagrams to trace references! Assignment (=) copies references, NOT objects.
